title: Gin It Up!

jokes.text: Ben, your heart's in the right place. Your heart and your butt.
jokes.setup: Leslie seems to be giving Ben a sincere compliment about his heart being in the right place
jokes.punchline: She adds 'and your butt' making it about his physical positioning rather than his intentions
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that subverts the expectation of a heartfelt moment with physical comedy reference

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Your heart is really in the right place.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And your butt.

jokes.text: Crap on a turd!
jokes.setup: Character expressing frustration
jokes.punchline: Uses an absurdly redundant exclamation combining two similar words
jokes.explanation: Absurd pseudo-profanity that feels character-specific and economical

jokes.text: I'm Leslie Knope. Tell your parents to recall me.
jokes.setup: Recall committee is handing out gift bags with fake Leslie ads to elementary school kids
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of asking children to tell their parents to recall a politician
jokes.explanation: Satirizes political dirty tricks with the absurd image of children as political operatives

dialogue.speaker: Recall Committee Member
dialogue.line: Here you go kids, take these gift bags home to your parents.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what's in the gift bags?

dialogue.speaker: Recall Committee Member
dialogue.line: Recall Leslie Knope pamphlets and buttons.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're handing out anti-me propaganda to third graders?

dialogue.speaker: Recall Committee Member
dialogue.line: It's a grassroots campaign.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a grass-roots campaign using actual grass roots. You're using children!

jokes.text: Why don't you just put the 'Don't' in front of 'Recall Knope'? Yep, that's a much better idea.
jokes.setup: Leslie has overcomplicated a simple solution with question mark stickers
jokes.punchline: Someone points out the obvious better solution and Leslie immediately agrees
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie character moment - overthinking simple problems then readily accepting better solutions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, why didn't I just... do that?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Because you're Leslie Knope.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're so right. That's the obvious solution. I love it. Everyone, we're doing this instead.

jokes.text: Can I have these question mark stickers? Why? I want to put them on stop signs. April, no!
jokes.setup: April asks for leftover question mark stickers
jokes.punchline: She wants to vandalize stop signs to make them say 'Stop?' which could cause accidents
jokes.explanation: Perfect April character moment combining deadpan delivery with genuinely dangerous mischief

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Can I have the leftover question mark stickers?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why do you need question mark stickers?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm going to put them on stop signs so they say 'Stop?' You know, to make people question things.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, that could cause accidents.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Oh, we have a new policy. Parks can only be reserved for witch covens or slip-and-slide competitions.
jokes.setup: Professional doctor asks to reserve a park for medical event
jokes.punchline: April creates absurd fake policy with two completely unrelated categories
jokes.explanation: Perfectly April - bureaucratic obstruction disguised as helpful policy, with absurd specificity

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but according to Parks Department Policy 7-B, subsection 12, medical events can only be scheduled during months that contain the letter 'R', and the organizer must be either a licensed physician or a moderately attractive beaver. You're a doctor, so that checks out, but this month is July, which has no R in it.

jokes.text: Which one are you? Uh, slip-and-slide competition. Seriously? No.
jokes.setup: April forces the doctor to choose between witch coven or slip-and-slide
jokes.punchline: Doctor plays along then immediately admits she was lying
jokes.explanation: Nice beat showing the doctor's deadpan response to April's absurdity

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You have to choose: witch coven or slip-and-slide.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Um... witch coven?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Wrong answer. It's slip-and-slide.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Wait, I was just playing along. I don't actually believe in witches.

jokes.text: Why would you even compete in something like that? It doesn't matter.
jokes.setup: April asks follow-up question about the fake slip-and-slide competition
jokes.punchline: Doctor shuts down the absurd line of questioning with perfect dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing both April's absurd persistence and the doctor's exasperation

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So if someone were to slip and fall on a slide that wasn't real, would they still get hurt?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I'm not going to answer that.

jokes.text: Tom, this witch wants to reserve a park for her satanic ritual.
jokes.setup: April earlier mentioned witch covens as fake policy
jokes.punchline: She escalates to calling the medical professional a witch doing satanic rituals
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation of the witch coven joke with April's characteristic troublemaking

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're not a doctor, you're a witch doing satanic rituals.

jokes.text: Right. Smashing. Uh, yes, I'm Thomas Haverford. I sort of run the whole department.
jokes.setup: Tom introduces himself to attractive woman
jokes.punchline: Uses fake British accent and exaggerates his importance at work
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom behavior - putting on fake persona to impress women

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Tom Haverford, Director of Business and Revenue Optimization for the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I basically run this entire city. Well, the fun parts anyway.

jokes.text: Brilliant. The old 'D's without 'B's. Uh, we can definitely help. Yeah, Tom's your guy. He actually used to run the Parks Department in his home country of Russia.
jokes.setup: Tom is trying to impress Nadia and April decides to 'help'
jokes.punchline: April creates elaborate fake backstory about Tom being Russian parks administrator
jokes.explanation: Perfect April move - appears helpful but actually sabotages Tom with absurd lie

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Tom, I'm going to help you impress Nadia. I'm going to tell her you're a Russian parks administrator.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What? No, April, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Your name is Tomislav. You ran the parks system in Moscow for twelve years. You're very serious about it.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm not doing this.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You also have a scar from a bear attack. It's on your back, so no one can see it, which is why you never mention it.

jokes.text: This one is mental. She's joking, obviously. How could I be from Russia? Not with this spot-on British accent.
jokes.setup: April has claimed Tom is Russian, Tom needs to deflect
jokes.punchline: Tom doubles down on fake British accent while denying he's Russian
jokes.explanation: Tom's desperation leads him deeper into the fake accent while trying to seem credible

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Russian? Me? No, no, no. I'm not Russian. I'm British, mate. Born and raised in London, England.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The name's Tom. Tom Haverford. Very British. Tea and crumpets and all that.

jokes.text: Whoa. I am pretty sure you shouldn't have a weapon at work. Literally everything is a weapon, son. That folder, in my hands, is far deadlier than this bow in yours.
jokes.setup: Someone points out Ron has a bow and arrow at work
jokes.punchline: Ron matter-of-factly explains that a folder would be deadlier, implying his combat skills
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - deadpan delivery of genuinely intimidating claim about his abilities

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A folder would be much deadlier.

jokes.text: Oh, that's... probably true.
jokes.setup: Ron claims a folder would be deadlier than a bow in his hands
jokes.punchline: Other character reluctantly agrees, showing Ron's reputation for being dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat that sells Ron's intimidating presence

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: A folder would be deadlier than a bow in my hands.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: ...Yeah, that's fair.

jokes.text: You need that many pages to say, 'Give my stuff to my wife'?
jokes.setup: Ben and Leslie have a long, complicated will
jokes.punchline: Ron reduces it to absurdly simple terms showing his practical mindset
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron - cutting through bureaucratic complexity with blunt practicality

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A will? It's simple. You die, your stuff goes to people you like. You don't like them? Burn it.

jokes.text: Upon my death, all of my belongings shall transfer to the man or animal who has killed me.
jokes.setup: Ron reads his will from when he was eight years old
jokes.punchline: Child Ron created a will that rewards whoever kills him
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and perfectly Ron - shows his lifelong philosophy of survival of the fittest, with the absurdity that an 8-year-old wrote this

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've left my entire estate to whoever kills me.

jokes.text: What are these weird symbols? The man who kills me will know.
jokes.setup: Ben asks about symbols on Ron's childhood will
jokes.punchline: Ron cryptically suggests only his killer will understand the symbols
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up that doubles down on the dark premise with mysterious undertones

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What are these symbols?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Only my killer will understand them.

jokes.text: The three most useless jobs in the world are, in order, lawyer, congressman, and doctor.
jokes.setup: Ben suggests Ron talk to their lawyer about his will
jokes.punchline: Ron lists three professions as useless, including doctor (while talking to someone about legal documents)
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Ron's anti-government, self-reliant philosophy delivered with characteristic certainty

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't need a lawyer. Lawyers are useless. Doctors are useless. And accountants are useless.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron, you're literally talking to a lawyer right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Present company excepted.

jokes.text: Where is this lawyer you speak of?
jokes.setup: Ron just declared lawyers one of the three most useless jobs
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks for the lawyer when told his possessions will go to the government
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contradiction - Ron's hatred of government overrides his hatred of lawyers

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Lawyers are one of the three most useless jobs.

dialogue.speaker: Someone
dialogue.line: Your possessions will go to the government.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well, I want a lawyer.

jokes.text: All right, well, uh, motion to end this session early so nothing can get done. Second. Gahhh, you guys are the worst!
jokes.setup: Council has been tabling all of Leslie's bills
jokes.punchline: They openly admit to obstructionism by moving to end the session to prevent work
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of political obstructionism to its logical absurd conclusion

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: I move to end the session.

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: Second.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, we still have seventeen items on the agenda.

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Sorry, Knope. We're just looking for any possible way to get you recalled because we don't like you. It's nothing personal. It absolutely is personal. That is the definition of 'personal.'
jokes.setup: Council member admits they're targeting Leslie because they don't like her
jokes.punchline: Claims it's not personal, then Leslie points out that's literally the definition of personal
jokes.explanation: Good logical contradiction pointing out the absurdity of the claim

dialogue.speaker: Council Member
dialogue.line: It's not personal.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not personal? That's literally the definition of personal. Personal means it's about a person. You don't like me, the person. That's personal.

jokes.text: 'The definition of personal.'
jokes.setup: Leslie points out their contradiction about what's personal
jokes.punchline: Council member just repeats the phrase, showing they don't care about logic
jokes.explanation: Weak follow-up that just restates without adding anything new

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: But you just said it was personal!

dialogue.speaker: Council Member
dialogue.line: It's not personal, it's just business.

jokes.text: See you tonight. Hope you like tongue baths, you big, nasty fireman.
jokes.setup: Donna accidentally tweeted from official Parks account instead of personal
jokes.punchline: The extremely sexual message combined with fireman fetish
jokes.explanation: Appropriately cringey sexual content that drives the plot while being character-appropriate for Donna

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go home and have a drink with a firefighter.

jokes.text: Then there's a picture of a pair of lips and then a picture of an eggplant. I'm both confused, and if we're being totally honest, a little aroused.
jokes.setup: Tom describes the emoji in Donna's sexual tweet
jokes.punchline: Tom admits to being aroused by the inappropriate government tweet
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom inappropriate oversharing that adds to the cringe factor

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: There's a peach, an eggplant, a water droplet, and a little skull emoji. The skull emoji is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm not gonna lie, I'm a little turned on right now.

jokes.text: Not to give you too much of a peek into my personal life, but this could have been way worse.
jokes.setup: Donna's sexual tweet has caused a scandal
jokes.punchline: She implies her usual tweets are even more explicit
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that makes the scandal seem tame by comparison while staying true to Donna's character

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Y'all are overreacting. This is literally the tamest thing I've ever tweeted.

jokes.text: Tongue baths? Eggplants? Firemen? Those men are heroes who deserve respect! 9/11!
jokes.setup: Jamm is making political hay out of the sexual tweet scandal
jokes.punchline: Randomly invokes 9/11 as if it's relevant to the Twitter scandal
jokes.explanation: Satirizes politicians who invoke 9/11 inappropriately, with good character work for Jamm

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: This Twitter scandal is a disgrace to our city. We need to remember what happened on 9/11.

jokes.text: This will be blown way out of proportion! You have my word on it!
jokes.setup: Political scandal brewing over innocent mistake
jokes.punchline: Jamm promises to blow it out of proportion as if that's a good thing
jokes.explanation: Good political satire showing how politicians create scandals for personal gain

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'm going to blow this completely out of proportion.

jokes.text: Guard your buds, 'cause they're about to get nipped!
jokes.setup: Leslie says they're going to 'nip this in the bud'
jokes.punchline: Someone enthusiastically extends the flower metaphor
jokes.explanation: Forced pun that doesn't add much beyond extending the metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We're going to nip this in the bud.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Yes! And then we'll water it down, let it wilt, and throw it in the compost bin!

jokes.text: Pawnee is like a petri dish of weird diseases. Did you know you have a higher rate of the West Nile virus than the actual Western Nile?
jokes.setup: Doctor explaining why medical organization wants to come to Pawnee
jokes.punchline: Pawnee somehow has more West Nile virus than the region it's named after
jokes.explanation: Good absurd statistic that reinforces Pawnee's reputation for dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: We want to establish a regional office here in Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's wonderful! Why Pawnee?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Pawnee somehow has more West Nile virus than the region it's named after.

jokes.text: Sorry, West Nile, looks like Pawnee wins again. What else have you beaten the Western Nile in? Cricket. Uh...
jokes.setup: Tom tries to make disease rates sound like a positive achievement
jokes.punchline: He can't think of anything else Pawnee beats the Western Nile region in
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's desperation to impress leading to absurd boasting about disease rates

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so Pawnee has the highest disease rate in the state. But look, we're number one at something. We're beating the entire Western Nile region in... in...

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: ...disease rates.

jokes.text: just fill out these forms, and we will be all set like two biscuits inside a tin.
jokes.setup: Tom is trying to sound British and sophisticated
jokes.punchline: Uses completely nonsensical British-sounding idiom
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's fake accent with an invented idiom that sounds vaguely British

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm going to sound very British and sophisticated right now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's absolutely brilliant, innit? Bob's your uncle, Queen's gambit, fish and chips, Big Ben.

jokes.text: I mean, obviously, accountants are a little more bad boy, but, uh, there's a respect there.
jokes.setup: Ben comparing himself to a lawyer
jokes.punchline: Claims accountants are 'bad boy' compared to lawyers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ben character moment - his earnest nerdiness about the most boring professions

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I mean, compared to lawyers, accountants are the bad boys. We're out here just living on the edge, doing whatever we want. Last week I wore a polo shirt with khakis. A polo shirt!

jokes.text: I've been talking to Ron about estate planning, and he is here to do some 'Good Will Hunting.' Because he has to draw up a new will, so--
jokes.setup: Ben introducing Ron to the lawyer
jokes.punchline: Makes forced pun connecting 'Good Will Hunting' movie to legal wills
jokes.explanation: Forced pun that's very much in character for Ben but doesn't land well

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron, I'd like you to meet our estate planning lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Pleasure.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I told him about your legal needs. Guess you could say we're hunting for some good will.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's the worst thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: Yes, I understood, Mr. Wyatt. Thank you. As I've said before, I just don't like puns.
jokes.setup: Ben makes terrible pun about wills
jokes.punchline: Lawyer flatly states he doesn't like puns, implying this isn't the first time
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man reaction that establishes this is a pattern with Ben

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I guess you could say making a will is all about having the write idea.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: I don't like puns.

jokes.text: Oh, come on, Trevor. Where there's a 'Will,' there's a way.
jokes.setup: Lawyer says he can't help without information, Ben wants to encourage him
jokes.punchline: Another forced pun playing on 'will' document vs 'will' as determination
jokes.explanation: Even more forced pun showing Ben can't help himself despite lawyer's stated dislike

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: I can't help you without more information.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You just need to have the will to do it.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Sir, I literally need your will. The legal document.

jokes.text: I'm gonna say this one last time, Wyatt. Check the accountant crap at the door. Yes, sir, I will. Unintentional.
jokes.setup: Lawyer angrily tells Ben to stop the puns
jokes.punchline: Ben accidentally makes another pun with 'will' and sheepishly admits it
jokes.explanation: Good escalation where Ben's pun addiction is so bad he can't even stop when trying to stop

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Ben, stop with the puns! I'm serious.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I know you are. And don't worry - I have the will to stop.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh no... I did it again, didn't I?

jokes.text: Well, it's actually quite simple. Uh, a Parks and Rec employee forgot to log out of the Parks and Rec Twitter account, they posted a personal, of the Parks and Rec private message by accident
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to explain the Twitter scandal clearly
jokes.punchline: Gets tongue-tied and creates confusing word salad
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's nervousness leading to verbal stumbling, though not particularly strong

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so what happened was I tweeted about the pothole on Ramsett Street, but then it got retweeted by the bot account, which made it look like I was endorsing the thing about the... the thing... and now everyone thinks I hate... infrastructure? No wait, I LOVE infrastructure. I'm very pro-infrastructure. But the tweet made it sound like I was anti-infrastructure, which is basically anti-Pawnee, which makes me anti-government, which technically makes me a libertarian, and you KNOW how I feel about libertarians!

jokes.text: Alright, sadly, due to your 'intracksidence'-- Not a word.
jokes.setup: Jamm trying to sound official and important
jokes.punchline: Makes up nonsensical word, Leslie immediately corrects him
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Jamm's incompetence and Leslie's quick corrections

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: As per the municipal code, we need to follow proper protoculation.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not a word.

jokes.text: When I was four, I thought that chocolate milk came from brown cows, and then I flip-flopped when I found out that there was something called chocolate syrup.
jokes.setup: Jamm accuses Leslie of flip-flopping on her explanation
jokes.punchline: Leslie uses childhood innocence about chocolate milk as analogy for learning new information
jokes.explanation: Clever analogy that's both logical and charmingly innocent, very Leslie

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: You keep changing your story. First you said one thing, now you're saying another.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Okay, well, when I was a kid, I thought chocolate milk came from brown cows. Then I learned it actually comes from regular cows, and they add chocolate. Did that make me a liar? No. It made me a learner. And that's what I'm doing here — learning and growing.

jokes.text: I don't think I'm out of line when I say that this scandal makes Benghazi look like whitewater! It's utterly meaningless.
jokes.setup: Jamm trying to make Twitter scandal sound important
jokes.punchline: Compares it to major political scandals then immediately says it's meaningless
jokes.explanation: Good political satire showing how politicians inflate minor issues while contradicting themselves

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: This Twitter scandal is just like Watergate, Iran-Contra, and the Monica Lewinsky affair combined.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: But ultimately, it's completely meaningless and nobody cares.

jokes.text: Perhaps he or she-- Or them. It could be a bunch of hot ladies.
jokes.setup: Trying to be inclusive about gender pronouns in investigation
jokes.punchline: Someone derails it into sexual fantasy about multiple women
jokes.explanation: Appropriately inappropriate for the character type but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We should be inclusive about gender pronouns in this investigation.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, absolutely. She, her, hers... them, they, theirs... and also me with multiple women at the same time.

jokes.text: What happened to your accent? Oh, yeah. It's gone. Huh. You know, I had a cold recently. I think that may have affected my voice, like, in certain British ways.
jokes.setup: Nadia notices Tom's British accent has disappeared
jokes.punchline: Tom gives absurd excuse about cold affecting his voice 'in British ways'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Tom desperately tries to maintain his lie with ridiculous explanation

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: Wait, where's your British accent? It's completely gone.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, that? Yeah, I caught a cold. It affects your voice in very British ways. The accent just... evaporates. It's science.

jokes.text: You have a cold? Can I feel your glands? Sure. Wow, you are wearing a lot of moisturizer on your throat. Best way to prevent crow's neck.
jokes.setup: Tom claims to have a cold, Nadia offers to check as a doctor
jokes.punchline: She discovers he's wearing excessive moisturizer, he gives vain excuse about preventing wrinkles
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Tom's vanity being exposed by medical professional, very character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: Let me take a look at you.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: You don't have a cold. You're wearing moisturizer. A lot of moisturizer.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, wrinkles are a sign of aging, and I'm not getting any younger.

jokes.text: Is this glitter?
jokes.setup: Nadia already discovered Tom's excessive moisturizer
jokes.punchline: Now finds glitter on his neck too
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that reveals even more about Tom's elaborate grooming routine

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: Now there's glitter on his neck too.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's a moisturizer with subtle shimmer. It's for men.

jokes.text: I am in love with Nadia Whatever-her-last-name-is, and I'm gonna keep her here, using the most powerful weapon I have-- bureaucratic incompetence.
jokes.setup: Tom confesses his strategy in talking head
jokes.punchline: Plans to use government inefficiency as a dating strategy
jokes.explanation: Great line that combines Tom's romantic desperation with commentary on government dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm gonna use government inefficiency as a dating strategy. See, I'll take a girl out on a date, and then I'll say, 'You know what? The government is so inefficient, it takes them six months to process a marriage license.' She'll be like, 'That's crazy!' And I'll be like, 'Yeah, so we might as well just skip all that and go straight to my apartment.' It's foolproof.

jokes.text: Oh, no. You filled out Form 3208. You were supposed to fill out Form 3248. Oh, God. This is to lease a hangar at the airport.
jokes.setup: Tom is deliberately creating bureaucratic obstacles
jokes.punchline: The wrong form is for something completely unrelated (airport hangar)
jokes.explanation: Good execution of Tom's plan with appropriately absurd government form confusion

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You need to fill out Form 27-B to proceed with your request.

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: What's Form 27-B?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's the application for hangar space at the regional airport.

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: But I'm not trying to store a plane.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you should've thought of that before you decided to exist in Pawnee.

jokes.text: Someone left out a blender, fresh fruits, and rum? I guess we gotta, like, make Daiquiris now. I like to have music on while I work, you know?
jokes.setup: Tom continues stalling Nadia with increasingly elaborate distractions
jokes.punchline: Pretends finding party supplies is a coincidence requiring immediate action
jokes.explanation: Escalates Tom's desperate stalling with increasingly obvious planted distractions

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Is that... are those party supplies in aisle seven? I think I see streamers. We should probably check on those immediately. For safety reasons.

jokes.text: In a shocking twist to an already juicy story, we have obtained access to Ms. Meagle's account, via a third party. Same hairdresser.
jokes.setup: Reporter dramatically announces they got access to private Twitter account
jokes.punchline: Reveals the 'third party' is just their shared hairdresser
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of dramatic news reporting with mundane reality of small-town gossip

dialogue.speaker: News reporter
dialogue.line: We've obtained access to a private Twitter account through a third party.

dialogue.speaker: News reporter
dialogue.line: That third party is our shared hairdresser.

jokes.text: Damn it, Typhoon! Why'd you do this? Maybe if you had bothered to come to my Great Gatsby Brunch, you'd know.
jokes.setup: Donna confronts her hairdresser about betraying her privacy
jokes.punchline: Hairdresser reveals it's revenge for missing a themed brunch party
jokes.explanation: Perfect small-town drama escalation with the absurdly specific revenge motivation

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: You told everyone at the community center about my bunions?

dialogue.speaker: Hairdresser
dialogue.line: That's right. And you know why? Because you didn't come to my themed brunch party.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: A themed brunch?

dialogue.speaker: Hairdresser
dialogue.line: It was Mimosas and Memes. You broke my heart, Donna.

jokes.text: Holy fuck.
jokes.setup: Lawyer sees Ron's actual wealth amount
jokes.punchline: Professional lawyer completely breaks character with profanity
jokes.explanation: Great reaction that shows just how wealthy Ron is through the lawyer's shocked response

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: According to these documents, your net worth is... Holy shit.

jokes.text: Is this a joke? Another word for 'jokes' is 'lies.' I do not lie. Therefore, I do not joke.
jokes.setup: Lawyer can't believe Ron's wealth figure
jokes.punchline: Ron explains his philosophy that jokes are lies and he doesn't lie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment that perfectly encapsulates Ron's literal, honest worldview with perfect logic

dialogue.speaker: Trevor
dialogue.line: You're telling me you have three million dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's correct.

dialogue.speaker: Trevor
dialogue.line: How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't spend money on frivolous things. I don't go to restaurants, I don't take vacations, I don't own a computer. I just work and save. It's a very simple philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Trevor
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's actually very impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Also, jokes are lies told in a social setting. I don't tell jokes, so people assume I'm very wealthy. I am.

jokes.text: I was right not to be threatened by you.
jokes.setup: Ron dismisses the lawyer after getting frustrated
jokes.punchline: Delivers devastating insult with complete calm
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron dismissal that's both insulting and completely matter-of-fact

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You're a public servant, but you serve the public poorly. I've seen more competence in a sloth sanctuary. Now get out of my office.

jokes.text: Annoying-ass Leslie has given me another annoying-ass task. ♪stickers, ♪bitchboss.
jokes.setup: Reading Donna's tweets about Leslie
jokes.punchline: Extremely blunt criticism with hashtags that make it worse
jokes.explanation: Good cringe content that drives the emotional stakes of the episode

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Leslie Knope is incompetent and her ideas are terrible. #ParksAndRec #DonnaKnowsBest

jokes.text: Twitter poll: On a scale of 98 to 100, how annoying is Leslie Knope? Cuz u know it ain't less than 98. All my #trueblood fans know what's up.
jokes.setup: Another mean tweet about Leslie
jokes.punchline: Creates poll with no option below 98% annoying, adds random True Blood reference
jokes.explanation: Escalates the meanness while adding absurd pop culture reference that's very Donna

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A poll? About me? What are the options?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: 98% annoying, 99% annoying, or 100% annoying. Also, you're basically Sookie Stackhouse but less useful.

jokes.text: Leslie, sometimes you're kind of annoying. I mean, I thought that was your thing. My thing? My thing is not being annoying.
jokes.setup: Donna tries to defend her tweets
jokes.punchline: Suggests being annoying is Leslie's defining characteristic, Leslie is horrified
jokes.explanation: Good character conflict moment that reveals how differently they see Leslie

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'm not annoying. Annoying is your thing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No it's not!

jokes.text: My things are making friendship bracelets and dancing like nobody's watching, thinking up really cool nicknames for my friends. You of all people should know that, El Diablo!
jokes.setup: Leslie defends what she thinks her 'things' are
jokes.punchline: Lists extremely Leslie activities, calls Donna by a nickname that contradicts her point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie response that's both earnest and accidentally proves the point about her quirky behavior

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: My things are community organizing, local government, and breakfast foods. I'm passionate about these things.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Leslie, those are just regular things.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, they're MY things. Like, I organized the Harvest Festival, I got the Rededication of the Pit off the ground, I invented the concept of 'Treat Yo Self' day—

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: You didn't invent that.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, not invent, but I perfected it. And waffles, Donna. Waffles are my thing. I've had waffles for breakfast for the past 2,000 days straight.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: That's not a personality trait, that's a cry for help.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're just jealous because your thing is being mean, and nobody wants that thing, Saucy Mama.

jokes.text: I think that you ask a lot of the people that you work with, and I think that people do what you ask because they love you. But, I also think that driving people as hard as you do can ruffle some feathers. I think a lot of things. I like thinking. And racquetball.
jokes.setup: Chris giving thoughtful analysis of Leslie's management style
jokes.punchline: Trails off into random personal facts about himself
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Chris's tendency to overshare and get distracted

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Leslie's management style is really quite effective. She leads with passion and optimism, which creates a positive work environment. She empowers her team members to take ownership of their projects, and she has this infectious enthusiasm that makes people want to work harder. She's also very detail-oriented and follows up on everything. I've been doing a lot of CrossFit lately, by the way. I'm up to 47 pull-ups in a single set. I also recently discovered this organic quinoa blend that has completely changed my approach to macronutrient distribution, and I'm only eating foods with a pH level above 7.

jokes.text: It appears, whereas 'bitch boss' is clearly an indication of her frustration, 'boss bitch' is a term of endearment. Isn't language fun? It's like racquetball for your mouth!
jokes.setup: Chris analyzing the linguistic difference between Donna's hashtags
jokes.punchline: Makes bizarre analogy between language and racquetball
jokes.explanation: Great Chris moment combining his earnest analysis with completely absurd analogy

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, Donna, the way you use hashtags is fascinating from a linguistic standpoint. It's almost like you're creating your own dialect.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I know what I'm doing, Chris.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm sure you do, but it's like... language is a lot like racquetball. You've got your walls, your angles, your velocity. And hashtags are the ball. You're just whacking them around with no regard for court positioning or basic strategy.

jokes.text: What did you name your imaginary airline? 'Jet Blue Ivy.' I figure Jay-Z and Beyonce's kid will own her own airline eventually.
jokes.setup: April asks about Tom's made-up airline name
jokes.punchline: Tom combines Jet Blue with Blue Ivy Carter's name, plus absurd prediction
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that's very Tom - combining pop culture with business speculation

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What's your airline called?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's called Jet Blue Ivy. It's a combination of Jet Blue and Blue Ivy Carter. And I'm predicting right now that we're gonna have a really good year.

jokes.text: Maybe I can drive you around and we'll pick another one? Maybe I'll bring a picnic lunch. Do you like tamales?
jokes.setup: Tom has sabotaged Nadia's park permit
jokes.punchline: Immediately offers elaborate alternative involving personal time together
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's transparent attempt to turn his sabotage into a date

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I may have made a tiny adjustment to your permit application. But I have a solution. How about instead of a park, we spend some quality time together? I'm thinking dinner, maybe some wine, I'll wear my good jeans.

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: Tom, you sabotaged my permit?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Sabotaged is such a strong word. I prefer 'strategically redirected.' But yes, now you're free to spend time with me instead.

jokes.text: I probably shouldn't say this, as a doctor. It's probably not cool. But I really hope you all get West Nile virus.
jokes.setup: Nadia is fed up with the bureaucratic runaround
jokes.punchline: Professional doctor wishes disease on government workers
jokes.explanation: Great dark humor that callbacks to earlier joke while showing Nadia's justified frustration

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: I've been trying to get this permit for three months. Three months! I have a medical degree. I know diseases. And right now, I wish every single one of you government workers would catch the flu.

jokes.text: Oh, you're joking. Yes, son, I am. First joke ever. Don't care for it.
jokes.setup: Ron appears to have an emotional breakthrough about mortality
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was joking, then immediately rejects humor as a concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment that subverts expectations while staying true to character - claiming his first joke while rejecting the concept

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I was just messing with you. Emotions are stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron, you can't reject the entire concept of human emotion.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Watch me.

jokes.text: I will leave my children $50 apiece for the cab home from my funeral and a steak dinner, end of discussion.
jokes.setup: Ron explains his philosophy about not spoiling his children
jokes.punchline: Specific, practical allowance that's both caring and austere
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron philosophy - practical, caring in his own way, but uncompromising

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't believe in coddling my children. I give them a small allowance—five dollars a week—which teaches them the value of money while ensuring they never have enough to buy anything fun.

jokes.text: I'd never lose to a boar.
jokes.setup: Ben suggests Ron might be killed by a wild boar
jokes.punchline: Ron matter-of-factly states his confidence in boar combat
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character confidence - of course Ron would never lose to a boar

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have killed a boar. I have been killed by a boar. It's an endless cycle.

jokes.text: Wow, 5 whole percent. So, I guess you are gonna teach them a lesson. Oh, my God, that's 5%? That is a lot of money.
jokes.setup: Ben sarcastically comments on Ron giving kids 5%
jokes.punchline: Realizes 5% of Ron's fortune is actually enormous
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces just how wealthy Ron is through Ben's realization

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, hold on. 5% of Ron's fortune?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's like... that's still millions of dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ron, you can't give kids millions of dollars.

jokes.text: Ron, I'm-- I'm incredibly flattered. Ralph Piatkowski and his wife Helen. He's the Maitre D' at Mulligan's Steakhouse, and he knows me better than anyone.
jokes.setup: Ben thinks he's being chosen as guardian for Ron's children
jokes.punchline: Ron names the steakhouse maitre d' as first choice
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection that perfectly captures Ron's priorities - steakhouse staff over coworkers

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're naming me as guardian for your kids?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Actually, my first choice is the maitre d' at the steakhouse.

jokes.text: How dare you demean the value of the political points I'm scoring?
jokes.setup: Leslie accuses Jamm of scoring cheap political points
jokes.punchline: Jamm acts offended at the suggestion his political points are cheap
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jamm's shameless political opportunism

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're just scoring cheap political points.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Cheap? These points are expensive. I have a very high-priced consultant who helps me score them.

jokes.text: Tom wants me to tell you that all the screw-ups were my fault, but really, he kept messing up so that you would stay in Pawnee as long as possible because he is super into you.
jokes.setup: April is supposed to lie for Tom
jokes.punchline: Immediately tells the truth about Tom's sabotage plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect April move - appears to help while actually sabotaging with brutal honesty

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Tom didn't sabotage anything. He's too incompetent to pull off sabotage. He can barely sabotage his own life.

jokes.text: I'm just trying to figure out if acting that insane is, like, romantic, or totally scary. I'll go with romantic.
jokes.setup: Nadia processing Tom's bizarre behavior
jokes.punchline: Decides his desperate weirdness counts as romantic
jokes.explanation: Good character beat that resolves Tom's arc positively despite his ridiculous behavior

dialogue.speaker: Nadia
dialogue.line: You know what? Your desperate weirdness... I'm going to count that as romantic.

jokes.text: He's sweet, he's cool, and you're, like, way out of his league, so there's literally no risk for you at all here.
jokes.setup: Nadia asks April about Tom
jokes.punchline: April gives brutally honest assessment that's both insulting and reassuring
jokes.explanation: Perfect April logic - insults Tom while actually being helpful in her own way

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Tom is an idiot. But he's OUR idiot. And he's going to mess things up, but he'll do it with the best intentions and a weird smile on his face. So yeah, he's terrible. But he's also kind of great.

tmdb_overview: Leslie throws Ben a birthday bash but is forced to leave early for an emergency late-night filibuster. Tom tries to make some memories with his new girlfriend, Ron and Donna go hunting. April reconnects with Andy.
insight: Deadpan delivery carries a mid-tier episode through 70 scattered jokes without standout punchlines.
